data.title: 5 Ideal 'Safer' March Dividend Bloomberg Businessweek Buys
data.description:
Foreword

This article is from analysts at Bloomberg Intelligence who track 2,000 companies in industries from apparel and autos to finance and food, as reported in the December, 2025 Bloomberg Businessweek's 50 Companies to Watch.

How Bloomberg analysts collated their 2026 list

"The analysis combines contrarian views and catalysts for change such as new leadership, asset sales or acquisitions, and plans for products and services. When building the list, BI also focused on the potential effects of shifting US tariff policies, the race to lock down supplies of vital resources, the ongoing geopolitical conflicts, and the transformative effects of artificial intelligence."-Tim Craighead, Bloomberg Intelligence

Any collection of stocks is more clearly understood by using yield-based (dogcatcher) analysis. These 50 watchable firms are perfect for the dogcatcher process. Our March 17 data focused on 27 dividend payers. The full list of 50 is posted in the Afterword text of this article.

Happily, 17 of the 27 dividend-paying watchable companies (Dividend Focus Group) live up to the Dogcatcher "safer" qualification, showing free cash flow yields exceeding dividend yields. As of 3/17/26, they were: Electronic Arts (EA), Lam Research (LRCX), Constellation Energy, ACS Actividades (ACSAF), Agilent (A), Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), ArcelorMittal (MT), Emerson Electric (EMR), KION Group (KIGRY), OTP Bank (OTPBF), Samsung (SSNLF), Commonwealth Bank of Australia (CMWAY), Voya Financial (VOYA), Continental (CTTAY), Danske Bank (DNKEY), PT Telekom (TLK), and Western Midstream (WES). Many first-time investors regard this condition as an invitation to look closer.

The seventeen "safer" dividends showed free cash flow yield exceeding their dividend yield. Of those sixteen, five IDEAL also paid dividends from $1K invested greater than their single share price. The IDEAL 5 were: Continental, Aviva, PT Telekom, Danske Bank, and Western Midstream. Many first time buyers take this IDEAL signal as a "watch to buy" sign.

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Analysts Estimated 20.84% To 44.74% Net Gains From Ten Top 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus Companies To March, 2027

Three of the top ten 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus company stocks by yield were among the top ten gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. (They are tinted gray in the chart below). Thus, the yield-based forecast for these 2026 Bloomberg top dogs was graded by Wall St. Wizards as 30% accurate.

Estimated dividends from $1000 invested in each of the highest-yielding stocks plus their aggregated one-year analyst median target prices (as reported by YCharts) supplied the data. Note: target prices from less than two analysts were not counted. Thus, ten probable top yielders for March were:

Bloomberg Top Ten March Dividend Focus 2026 Dogs By Net Gains

Agilent Technologies was projected to net $477.38 based on a median of target price estimates from 20 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 31% over the market as a whole.

Nike (NKE) was projected to net $405.82 based on the median of target estimates from 29 analysts, plus its last dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 27% over the market as a whole.

Diageo (DEO) was projected to net $381.75, based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 8 brokers. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 54% less than the market as a whole.

Marvell Technology (MRVL) was projected to net $319.88 based on the median of target prices from 43 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 97% over the market as a whole.

PT Telekom Indonesia was projected to net $281.82, based on the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, plus annual dividend, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

Constellation Energy Corp. (CEG) was projected to net $278.07 based on dividends, plus the median of the target price estimates from 20 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for CEG.

Emerson Electric was projected to net $260.44 based on dividends, plus the median of target estimates from 328 brokers, less transaction fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 25% more than the market as a whole.

Voya Financial was projected to net $253.76 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 11 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% under the market as a whole.

Taiwan Semiconductor was projected to net $245.86 based on the median of target prices from 19 analysts, plus annual dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% more than the market as a whole.

Lam Research was projected to net $208.44, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 36 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 78% greater than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 30.83% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these ten stocks. These gain estimates were subject to average risk/volatility 6% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks are dividend dogs when they show three key qualities: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, and (2) their prices fall. Thus, (3) yield (dividend/price) grows higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection become valued as "dividend dogs." More specifically, they may be, in fact, best named "underdogs," owing to their contrarian nature.

38 of 47 Bloomberg March 2026 Dividend Focus Dogs Show Positive Annual Returns (9 More Show Losses)

23 of 47 Bloomberg March 2026 Dividend Focus Dogs Show Analyst Target Gains (7 Show Losses; 17 Are Unrated)

27 of 47 Bloomberg March 2026 Dividend Focus Dogs Show Positive Yields

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): 10 Top Bloomberg Dividend Focus Dogs By Yield

The top ten March Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus stocks by yield represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

One energy stock placed first, Western Midstream [1], while four financial services stocks occupied the second, fourth, ninth, and tenth places: Aviva [2], Danske Bank [4], Voya Financial [9], and Commonwealth Bank of Australia [10].

One communications stock placed third, PT Telecom Indonesia [3], and then three consumer discretionary stocks occupied the fifth, seventh, and eighth places: Sodexo SA [5], NIKE [8], and Continental [9]. Finally, one real estate member placed sixth, Keppel DC REIT (KPDCF) [6], to complete the top-ten March list of Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus stocks by yield.

Bloomberg Dividend Focus 2026 Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 3/17/26-7

The above chart reveals 27 Bloomberg Dividend Focus dogs out of 48 subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by its annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive divi-dog on the list is Western Midstream at $11.39 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive is Marvell Technology at $378.36 per dividend dollar.

Three of Twenty-seven Dividend Paying Bloomberg March 2026 Focus Dogs Show Negative Free-Cash-Flow Margins

Source: ycharts.com

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends. Three dividend paying Bloomberg stocks so infected are: Aviva, Keppel DC REIT, and Nucor. These are considered unsafe to buy because their cash flow yield is less than dividend yield.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, uncertainty, trade deals, wars, and taco-riffs, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Ten Top-Yield 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus Stocks Back to "Fair-Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, seven of the top ten are already ideally priced. Besides those already in the ideal zone, three more top-yield stocks are within 68% of getting there.

The alternative, of course, could be that Nike, Voya, and, Commonwealth split shares to conform to the Dogcatcher ideal. That may be too much to ask in these highly disrupted, inflationary, recessionary, yet cash-rich, and volatile times. Mr. Market is far more effective at moving prices up or down to appropriate amounts. Just watch, then buy, when your targeted stock-price hits the sweet spot. Give it a few more weeks.

Source: ycharts.com

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr Targets Showed 21.38% To 44.83% Top Ten 2026 Bloomberg Upsides To March 2027; (32) Two -15.48% to -49.94% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 10.53% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 Bloomberg 2026 Focus Stocks to March, 2027

Ten-top Focus Stocks were culled by yield for this monthly update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

Source: ycharts.com

As noted above, top ten 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus stocks selected 3/17/26 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. Consumer discretionary, health care, materials, technology, industrials, and utilities, missed the top-ten cut.

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expect 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield Dividend Bloomberg 2026 Stocks (34) To Deliver 12.25% Vs. (35) 13.69% Net Gains by All Ten Come March, 2027

$5,000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten 2026 Dividend Focus are analyst 1-year targets to deliver 10.53% LESS gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The eighth lowest-priced top-ten yielding stock, NIKE, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 40.58%.

The five lowest-priced top-yield 2026 Dividend Focus dogs for March were: Keppel DC REIT; Continental; Aviva; Sodexo; PT Telekom, with prices ranging from $1.95 to $18.24.

Five higher-priced 2026 Dividend Focus top-yield dogs as of December 17 were: Danske Bank, Western Midstream; NIKE; Voya; Commonwealth Bank of Australia, with prices ranging from $25.06 to $125.46.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains, based on analyst targets, added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the euphoria of the good-time markets cools and the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into mid-2026, with the exception of a few meme stocks, look for most of the top ten 2026 Bloomberg Focus Dividend Dogs to become fairly priced… slowly, but surely, or very suddenly.

Afterword

Buy 5 Ideal of 17 'Safer' Bloomberg 2026 Focus Stocks

Mid-March 2026, 17 of the 47 Bloomberg 2026 Dividend Focus stocks were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields.

YCharts 2/9/26 data showed that five of fourteen IDEAL dividends (from $1,000 invested) exceeding single share price, were also "safer": Western Midstream, Danske Bank, PT Telekom, Aviva, and Continental.

How 'Safer' Dividend Vs. Price Data Produced "Fair Price" Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that five dividends from $1,000.00 invested in our top-ten 'safer' dividend-focused stocks conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or greater than) the price of a single share. Those five ideal dogs were Western Midstream, PT Telekom, Danske Bank, Continental, and KION Group.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) equal to or greater than the single share price of each stock—a so-called, "fair price."

A market correction of 64.44% could put all ten share prices of these "safer" survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors of these companies to raise dividends

Footnotes

The Full List of Bloomberg 50 Companies to Watch in 2026

Three stocks on the original Bloomberg 50 list were unavailable through YCharts sources. The listing above shows that one Chinese healthcare stock, one overseas bank, and one co-op insurance firm were not listed in YCharts; two were probably private enterprises.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher 2026 Bloomberg Dividend Focus dog updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net-gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" Bloomberg Watchlist dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

data.title: Nomura Wealth Builder Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market review

Despite significant bouts of volatility, global markets generally finished 4Q25 with positive returns. Expensive valuations and tight spreads amplified instances of underperformance and macro weakness. Those headwinds were offset, however, by nimble corporate actions to mitigate the impact of tariffs and by governments easing fiscal and monetary policy.

In October, markets continued to ride 3Q momentum, generated by strong earnings and improved US-China trade relations. The US Federal Reserve (Fed) cut rates and announced an end to quantitative tightening. Global fixed income and equities rallied in unison.

By the end of October, headwinds picked up that would remain for the rest of the year. First, the growing “circular” nature of artificial intelligence (AI) investment sparked hesitation across global equity markets. Second, budget disputes and plans for growing deficits inspired rate volatility, weighing on global fixed income markets. Finally, the US government shutdown restricted data availability, dividing the Fed and further adding to cross-asset volatility.

Global markets reversed course in November and risk assets suffered, including emerging markets, Bitcoin, and global technology stocks. Continued weakness in Chinese economic data also weighed on emerging market returns, despite growing AI advances. Later in the month, higher-quality areas of developed market equities and global fixed income recovered with mild gains and renewed hope for further US monetary easing.

Although equity markets outside the US had been strong all year, benefiting from the relative weakness of the dollar, US equities outperformed early in 4Q. In December, however, the dollar retreated, enabling European and emerging markets to post strong performance, once again outperforming US equities.

December rate cuts implemented by both the Fed and Bank of England were met with rising global bond yields, in part due to rising rates in Japan. Several segments of global fixed income markets suffered as a result, including investment grade corporates. Emerging market bonds outperformed, however, in keeping with their year-long strength.

Continued uncertainty and falling global policy rates continued to push gold higher, peaking in the last week of 4Q. Conversely, oil continued its decline due to abundant supply from OPEC+ and US producers, made worse by relatively softening global demand, offsetting blockades and sanctions on some oil producing countries like Venezuela.

Source: Bloomberg, unless otherwise indicated.

Within the Fund

For 4Q25, Nomura Wealth Builder Fund Institutional Class shares outperformed the Fund's benchmark, a blend of 60% S&P 500® Index and 40% Bloomberg US Aggregate Index.

The Fund's outperformance at the portfolio level was due to US exposure to quality and income and US large-cap value equities, which outperformed the benchmark. In fixed income, high yield exposure contributed slightly to outperformance. International equities outperformed the S&P 500 Index, but their overall portfolio impact was minimal with negative selection effects. Volatility over the reporting period weighed on tech and growth, while value-like strategies outperformed on a relative basis. International stocks underperformed the S&P 500 Index.

In fixed income, high yield corporate bonds outperformed relative to the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index. The investment grade portion of the Fund slightly underperformed the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index. Quality securitized fixed income outperformed over the period, contributing to the Bloomberg US Aggregate Index's relative performance.

We periodically examine the contribution of derivatives to the Fund's performance. Based on available information, the derivative positions did not materially affect performance.

Portfolio positioning

The Fund seeks to provide high current income and investments that have the potential for capital appreciation. The Fund's strategic policy weights reflect a commitment to diversification across geographies and asset classes.

During the quarter, we may adjust our exposures to reflect our convictions based on the changing economic environment. In 4Q, we decreased our allocation to fixed income in exchange for equity exposure. We added Zoetis Inc. (ZTS), Qnity Electronics Inc., and London Stock Exchange Group PLC (LNSTY) to the portfolio, while Kenvue Inc. (KVUE), Applied Materials Inc. (AMAT), Otis Worldwide Corp. (OTIS), Paychex Inc. (PAYX), and Starbucks Corp. (SBUX) were removed. At the same time, we added to our position in NVIDIA (NVDA). Fixed income duration remained stable throughout the period.

Outlook

AI-related investments have been volatile and the valuations of US equity indices remain elevated. While there are major obstacles to overcome and future catalysts to scrutinize, the outlook for companies with strong fundamentals remains

favorable. Some evidence of sector and region rotation is emerging, and moderate equity gains remain our base case. Given extended valuations in both value and growth, we take a balanced view on US equity factors, as well as on US versus international equities. In credit, spreads remain tight relative to history but still offer some carry for long-term investors. Even as US policy rates fall, volatility persists in the intermediate and long segments of the yield curve; there is little opportunity for significant fixed income outperformance, in our view.

Investing involves risk, including the possible loss of principal.

Market risk is the risk that all or a majority of the securities in a certain market – like the stock market or bond market – will decline in value because of factors such as adverse political or economic conditions, future expectations, investor confidence, or heavy institutional selling. • Fixed income securities can lose value, including the possible loss of principal. Fixed income securities are subject to credit risk and interest rate risk. Credit risk is the risk that an issuer of a fixed income security may be unable to make interest payments and/or repay principal in a timely manner. Interest rate risk is the risk that prices of bonds and other fixed income securities will increase as interest rates fall and decrease as interest rates rise. Fixed income securities with longer maturities or duration generally are more sensitive to interest rate changes. • Investments in derivatives may involve additional expenses and are subject to significant loss if they perform differently than anticipated. Derivatives are also subject to the risk that the counterparty may fail to perform its obligations. • A fund or strategy that invests in an exchange-traded fund (ETF) will typically be exposed to the risks of the instruments in which the ETF invests and will bear its proportionate share of the ETF's fees and expenses. • Foreign securities (particularly in emerging markets) may be adversely affected by political instability, changes in currency exchange rates, inefficient markets and higher transaction costs, foreign economic conditions, the imposition of economic or trade sanctions, or inadequate or different regulatory and accounting standards. • Governments or regulatory authorities may take actions that could adversely affect various sectors of the securities markets and affect performance. • High yield securities ("junk bonds") are subject to reduced creditworthiness of issuers, increased risk of default, and a more limited and less liquid secondary market. High yield securities may also be subject to greater price volatility and risk of loss of income and principal than higher-rated securities. • A fund that uses leverage may be more volatile and may be subject to a greater risk of loss than a fund that does not use leverage. • The fund or strategy is subject to liquidity risk, which is the risk that the fund or strategy's investments cannot be readily sold within seven calendar days at approximately the price at which the fund or strategy has valued them. • Investing in the real estate industry includes risks such as declines in real estate value, lack of availability of mortgage funds, overbuilding, extended vacancies, increases in property taxes, changes in zoning laws, costs from clean-up of environmental problems, uninsured damages, variations in rents, and changes in interest rates.
